content:
{
  "title": "Cross-Lingual Contrastive Prompting for Low-Resource Language Tasks",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) struggle with accuracy and consistency in low-resource languages due to limited training data and suboptimal prompt transfer. Current prompting methods do not fully leverage multilingual contexts to improve performance on such languages.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly deployed in multilingual applications, effective support for low-resource languages becomes essential for inclusivity and access. Recent research suggests LLMs possess latent multilingual capabilities that can be unlocked with the right prompting, but explicit prompt transfer and augmentation strategies are underexplored.",
  "method": "We propose Cross-Lingual Contrastive Prompting (CLCP): for each target low-resource language input, generate a parallel prompt in a high-resource language, then prompt the LLM with both (e.g., concatenated or in a structured format), encouraging implicit cross-lingual transfer. During inference, the LLM is instructed to produce an answer in the target language, grounded by the content and structure of the high-resource instance. Optionally, an auxiliary cross-lingual consistency loss can be applied via self-consistency prompting (with chain-of-thought or explanation steps) to encourage aligned reasoning.",
  "experiment_plan": "Evaluate CLCP on XTREME or FLORES-101 for text classification and QA tasks in low-resource languages (e.g., Swahili, Nepali). Compare against few-shot translation-based prompting and the strongest baseline: multilingual in-context learning with high-resource exemplars only. Use accuracy, F1, and BLEU (for generative tasks) as metrics. Statistical significance will be assessed over at least three random seeds."
}
